masthead.title_html: Reason Your Way Across College Football
masthead.title_plain: Reason Your Way Across College Football
masthead.dek: Twenty questions, and almost none of the answers are the first name that springs to mind. Each routes to a set you can walk — a program, a state, a coach, a decade, a count — with a filter or a twist that knocks out the obvious. Narrow the field, place your bet, then tap to check.
masthead.footer_html: Twenty questions across a dozen answer sets, built reflex-defeater-first — every fact web-verified and sourced. Records as of the 2024–25 season.

plan.note: Built with the routing engine: every answer comes from a right-sized walkable set (programs, states, coaches, decades, presidents, counts, stadiums, nicknames, positions, trophies), and is made non-obvious by a reflex-defeater or a slicer that removes the obvious answer. Difficulty spread: ~8 fair mediums, ~12 hard, no obscurity. Michigan (all-time wins leader) was unavailable — already used as an answer in the U.S. states set.

questions.q: Counting only the seasons since the year 2000, which program has won the most national championships?
questions.detail: Six national titles since 2000 — 2009, 2011, 2012, 2015, 2017, and 2020.
questions.why: Six championships in two decades is more than any other program managed; the 'since 2000' slice strips away the old blue-blood totals and leaves the modern dynasty.
questions.more: No one else reached more than three in that window — Clemson and Ohio State sit in the next tier.
questions.source: Wikipedia (Alabama Crimson Tide football national championships); CBS Sports.

questions.q: Which program has won more games than any other team that has never won a national championship?
questions.detail: About 787 all-time wins — the most of any program that has never claimed a recognized national title.
questions.why: The slice removes every champion, so the answer is a long-running power with no ring rather than a famous blue blood; ~787 wins ranks around 23rd overall.
questions.more: Virginia Tech is just behind at roughly 778; West Virginia's closest brush was an undefeated 1988 regular season.
questions.source: ESPN (winningest teams without a national title); CBS Sports all-time wins.

questions.q: Players from which school have won the Heisman Trophy more times than from any other?
questions.detail: Eight Heisman winners — the most of any school, after Reggie Bush's 2005 award was reinstated in 2024.
questions.why: Most people guess Notre Dame or Ohio State, but USC's tradition of star tailbacks and quarterbacks puts it clear at eight.
questions.more: Ohio State, Oklahoma, and Notre Dame are tied next at seven apiece.
questions.source: NCAA.com; ESPN (most Heisman winners by school).

questions.q: Only one player has ever won the Heisman Trophy twice. What school did he play for?
questions.detail: Archie Griffin won as a junior and senior, in 1974 and 1975 — still the only two-time winner.
questions.why: In ninety years of the award, no one else has repeated; many great players (Tebow, Manziel) tried and fell short the second time.
questions.more: Griffin rushed for about 1,695 yards in his first Heisman season.
questions.source: Ohio State Athletics; Britannica (Archie Griffin).

questions.q: Which head coach won more games than anyone else in major-college (FBS) football history?
questions.detail: 409 wins across 46 seasons, all at Penn State.
questions.why: Career wins reward sheer longevity, so the answer is the man who coached one school for four-plus decades — not the more famous Bear Bryant or Nick Saban.
questions.more: Bobby Bowden is next among modern coaches at 377; Bryant finished with 323. (John Gagliardi won more across all divisions, at Division III.)
questions.source: ESPN (winningest coaches all-time); NCAA FBS coaching records.

questions.q: Among coaches who led a major-college team for at least ten years, who has the best career winning percentage?
questions.detail: A .881 winning percentage (105–12–5) at Notre Dame in the 1910s and 1920s.
questions.why: A percentage record favors a short, nearly flawless early career over a long modern one, so the answer comes from a century ago, not the present day.
questions.more: Ryan Day has posted a higher raw figure (~.882) but lacks the ten-year minimum to qualify.
questions.source: College Football Hall of Fame (Rockne); NCAA coaching records.

questions.q: Which U.S. state is home to more top-division (FBS) football teams than any other?
questions.detail: Thirteen FBS programs — more than any other state.
questions.why: Its size and football fever support a deep bench of public and private programs; 13 teams clears the field.
questions.more: Ohio is next with eight; California, Florida, and North Carolina follow with seven each.
questions.source: 247Sports (top states for college football, 2024); BetMGM.

questions.q: The most-played rivalry in all of college football is between two small schools in the same state. Which state?
questions.detail: Lafayette vs. Lehigh, first played in 1884, reached its 161st meeting in 2025 — the most-played series in the sport. Both are in eastern Pennsylvania.
questions.why: 'Most-played' means oldest and never interrupted, not most famous — so it beats Army–Navy and the big-brand games; 161 meetings and counting.
questions.more: Army–Navy, by comparison, has been played about 125 times.
questions.source: NCAA.com (Lafayette–Lehigh all-time history, 2025); Lehigh University News.

questions.q: Leaving out the Big Ten, which school plays in the largest football stadium?
questions.detail: Kyle Field seats 102,733 — the largest stadium outside the Big Ten, and the largest in the SEC.
questions.why: The slice removes the famous Big Ten giants (Michigan, Penn State, Ohio State), leaving the SEC's biggest cathedral; 102,733 seats.
questions.more: It edges LSU's Tiger Stadium (102,321) for the SEC lead.
questions.source: Athlon Sports (largest stadiums, 2024); Sportskeeda.

questions.q: After Texas and Oklahoma left in 2024, which school now has the largest stadium in the Big 12?
questions.detail: LaVell Edwards Stadium, about 62,000 — the largest in the realigned 16-team Big 12.
questions.why: The two departed schools owned the league's biggest houses, so the title passed to an unexpected newcomer out West; ~62,000 seats.
questions.more: Iowa State's Jack Trice Stadium is next at 61,500.
questions.source: KSL Sports (BYU largest Big 12 stadium, 2024).

questions.q: Which SEC school plays its home games in the smallest town?
questions.detail: Starkville, Mississippi — population about 24,360 — one of the smallest host towns of any power-conference program.
questions.why: Among big-time programs in big stadiums, the answer is the most rural, isolated campus; Starkville has fewer than 25,000 residents.
questions.more: Even Pullman, Washington — long cited as tiny — is bigger, at about 32,900.
questions.source: U.S. Census via Wikipedia (Starkville, MS; Pullman, WA).

questions.q: The very first college football game was played between two New Jersey schools, 25 players to a side, under soccer-style rules. In which decade?
questions.detail: Rutgers beat the College of New Jersey (Princeton) 6–4 on November 6, 1869.
questions.why: Reason from the era — the dawn of the sport, just after the Civil War — and you land in the 1860s, even though the game looks far older.
questions.more: Each side fielded 25 players that day; the modern eleven came later.
questions.source: Wikipedia (1869 Princeton vs. Rutgers game); Rutgers Athletics.

questions.q: After a season so deadly that 18 players died, a U.S. president called Harvard, Yale, and Princeton to the White House and pushed the reforms that brought the forward pass and the group that became the NCAA. Who was that president?
questions.detail: Roosevelt convened the meeting that led to legalizing the forward pass, banning the deadly mass formations, and founding what became the NCAA.
questions.why: Picture the hands-on, outdoorsy president of the deadly mass-formation era — before helmets and the forward pass — and it's the Rough Rider; 18 deaths forced his hand.
questions.more: The reform meeting and rule changes came in 1905–06.
questions.source: History.com and Washington Post (“How Teddy Roosevelt saved football”).

questions.q: In the most lopsided game in college football history, coach John Heisman's Georgia Tech overwhelmed an undermanned Cumberland team. How many points did Georgia Tech score?
questions.detail: Georgia Tech won 222–0 on October 7, 1916 — still the most points and the most lopsided result ever.
questions.why: The only sane move is to guess absurdly high: Tech scored 222 and led 126–0 at halftime.
questions.more: Georgia Tech put up 63 points in the first quarter alone.
questions.source: Wikipedia (1916 Cumberland vs. Georgia Tech game); NCAA.com.

questions.q: The longest winning streak in major college football history was built by Bud Wilkinson's Oklahoma teams in the 1950s, before Notre Dame finally ended it 7–0. How many games long was the streak?
questions.detail: Oklahoma won 47 straight from 1953 to 1957 — still the FBS record.
questions.why: An all-time streak spans several full seasons, so reason past the usual 20s and 30s; this one reached 47 before it broke.
questions.more: Notre Dame ended it with a 7–0 upset in Norman on November 16, 1957.
questions.source: Wikipedia (longest NCAA Division I winning streaks); ESPN.

questions.q: Florida State holds the record for the most seasons in a row reaching a bowl game. About how many straight seasons was the streak?
questions.detail: Thirty-six consecutive bowl seasons — the longest such run in college football history.
questions.why: It bridged both of the Seminoles' national-title years, so reason that a dynasty-length run lands in the mid-30s, not the low 20s.
questions.more: The streak covered the 1993 and 1999 championship seasons before it ended.
questions.source: ESPN (longest bowl game streaks); Sports Illustrated.

questions.q: All-time, which position has produced the most Heisman Trophy winners?
questions.detail: Running backs lead the all-time count, narrowly ahead of quarterbacks (about 40 to 39 as of 2024).
questions.why: Recent winners are almost all quarterbacks, but the award is ninety years old and its first six decades belonged to ball-carriers, so running back still edges ahead.
questions.more: Only four wide receivers have ever won: Tim Brown, Desmond Howard, DeVonta Smith, and Travis Hunter (2024). The QB count is closing the gap fast.
questions.source: ESPN (positions with the most Heisman Trophies, 2024).

questions.q: Among the roughly 130 schools in college football's top division, which animal nickname is shared by the most teams?
questions.detail: Five FBS schools are the Tigers — Auburn, Clemson, LSU, Memphis, and Missouri.
questions.why: Picture the most common animal on a major-college helmet and count the SEC alone; no other FBS nickname is held by as many programs.
questions.more: About nineteen different nicknames are shared by at least two FBS teams.
questions.source: BroBible (most common FBS nicknames); Deseret News mascot survey.

questions.q: What is the name of the oldest rivalry trophy in major college football — a plain container one team left behind after a game in 1903?
questions.detail: A five-gallon water jug Michigan left at Minnesota after their 1903 game (a 6–6 tie); the schools have played for it ever since.
questions.why: People reach for a grand-sounding cup, but the oldest trophy is a humble household jug — in play since 1903.
questions.more: Michigan had brought its own water, reportedly fearing the host wouldn't supply a clean supply, and left the jug behind.
questions.source: Wikipedia (Little Brown Jug, college football trophy); Michigan Today.

questions.q: At one school, players rub a rock for luck and then sprint down a hill into the stadium before every home game. Which school?
questions.detail: Players touch Howard's Rock atop The Hill at Memorial Stadium — a stadium also nicknamed 'Death Valley.'
questions.why: Two stadiums go by 'Death Valley,' but the rock-and-hill entrance points to the Tigers in South Carolina, not LSU; the rock tradition dates to the mid-1960s.
questions.more: The rock was carried back from the real Death Valley in California and given to coach Frank Howard.
questions.source: Wikipedia (Howard's Rock); ESPN.
